segments.s438-g49: This is a compressed, poetic word order: the prepositional phrase 'به چنگ آیدش' (literally 'to the claw comes-to-him') comes before the nominative nouns 'گور و آهو'. 'آیدش' is the verb آید plus the enclitic -ش ('to him/for him'), so the phrase means 'fall into his clutches' or 'come to his claw'. 'به دشت' is a locative adjunct 'on/in the plain' placed after the noun phrase.
segments.s438-g50: The clause begins with the demonstrative/ablative 'ازان' ('from/that'), followed by 'دشت' ('plain') described by the adjective 'خرم' (here 'lush/pleasant'). 'نشاید' is an archaic negative modal meaning 'it is not fitting/possible', and it governs the verbal noun/infinitive 'گذشت' ('to pass' / 'passage') — together 'نشاید گذشت' = 'one cannot pass'.
segments.s438-g51: 'ز گفتار او' is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of his speech'. The subject 'رستم' follows and is followed by the past verb 'آمد' used idiomatically with the prepositional complement 'به شور' ('into passion/ardor' = became enraged). Note the order: causal phrase → subject → verb → complement.
segments.s438-g52: 'ازان دشت' ('that plain') is then characterized by two coordinated modifiers 'پرآب و نخچیرگور' — 'پرآب' ('well-watered, full of water') and 'نخچیرگور' (a compound noun meaning 'grave-robber'). The adjective phrase follows the head noun, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s438-g53: 'به چیزی که آید کسی را' is a conditional/temporal clause: 'when/if something happens to someone.' 'به' introduces the affected event, 'چیزی' is 'something', 'که آید' is a relative clause 'that happens', and 'کسی را' marks the person affected (را here marks the definite/affected object). 'زمان' (time) is then the subject that will govern the following verb.
segments.s438-g54: This gives the consequence: 'بپیچد دلش' — 'his heart is twisted/confused' — where 'بپیچد' is a present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن used to express a potential/result and comes before the clitic-marked possessive 'دلش' ('his heart'). Then 'کور گردد گمان' places the subject 'گمان' (belief/guess) after the verb phrase 'کور گردد' ('becomes blind'), a typical poetic inversion: 'the conjecture becomes blind.'
segments.s438-g55: 'چنین است' is a fixed phrase 'thus is' or 'such is'. 'کار جهان جهان' names the subject — 'the work/way of the world' — with the second 'جهان' repeating the word for poetic emphasis (a common rhetorical device in classical Persian).
segments.s438-g56: 'نخواهد گشادن' is the negative future/optative construction 'will not open/reveal'. The infinitive 'گشادن' follows the auxiliary 'نخواهد'. 'بمابر' is an old/poetic locative/benefactive 'for us' (or 'to us'), and 'نهان' ('hidden') is the object: 'will not open/reveal the hidden things to us.'
segments.s438-g81: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic). The clause has subject-pronoun او + predicate تنگ شد: تنگ (adjective 'narrow/cramped') + شد (past of شدن 'become'), so literally 'he became cramped'. در میان دو چاه is a prepositional phrase 'in/between two pits' (در میان = between). The word order (pronoun – predicate – locative phrase) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s438-g82: ز is the poetic form of از 'from' indicating source ('out of'/'from the grip of...'). چنگ زمانه is a metaphorical genitive 'the claw/grip of Time/Fortune' (چنگ = claw/grip, زمانه = fortune/time). همی + جست forms an older continuous/frequentative past: همی (continuous particle) with past جست ('sought' / 'leapt') produces 'kept seeking/kept trying to find a way'. راه is the object 'a way/path'.
segments.s438-g83: دو پایش = 'his two feet' where -ش is the possessive suffix. فروشد here is an archaic/poetic spelling of فرو شد (past of فرو رفت / فروشد), meaning 'sank/fell in' — the verb is past and governs the locative phrase به یک چاهسار ('into a single pit/well-field'). Note that فروشد as printed has senses in the lexicon that don't match this verbal use, so it is treated as an inflected verb meaning 'sank/fell in'.
segments.s438-g84: نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'there was not / there did not remain' (negative of بودن). جای آویزش و کارزار is a noun phrase 'a place for hanging/suspension and for battle'; آویزش ('suspension, hanging') and کارزار ('battle/fighting') are coordinated by و. The whole line means there was no room either to be suspended/hung or to fight.
segments.s438-g85: بن چاه = 'the bottom/base of the pit' (بن = bottom/base + چاه = pit/well). پر here is the adjective 'full (of)' and takes a following genitive list حربه و تیغ تیز = 'weapons and sharp swords'. Word order is noun (location) + predicate adjective 'full of' + list of items filling it.
segments.s438-g86: نبد again negates existence 'there was not'. جای مردی و راه گریز = 'a place for a man and a way of escape' (مردی = a man; راه گریز = escape-route). The coordinated nouns (جای ... و راه گریز) are both ruled by the negation نبد, so the sense is 'there was neither place for a man nor any escape.'
segments.s438-g87: بدرید is the past of دریدن 'to tear/cut' ('they tore/cut'). پهلوی رخش = 'the flank/side of Rakhsh' (پهلوی = flank/side + رخش = the horse's name). سترگ in the text is problematic in the supplied lemma list (it gives senses 'eye/sight'), but here it functions adjectivally with رخش to mean 'mighty/large' (an epithet). The verb directly governs this object phrase.
segments.s438-g88: The second coordinated noun phrase (bound to the same verb بدرید) lists body parts: بر و پای آن پهلوان بزرگ. Here بر (an older noun for a limb/part; not the preposition sense) and پای 'foot/leg' are joined by و and followed by the genitive phrase آن پهلوان بزرگ 'of that great champion' — so the verb 'tore/cut' applies to both Rakhsh's flank and to the hero's limb(s).
segments.s438-g89: "به مردی" is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'manfully / like a man' (here describing manner). "تن خویش را" is the definite direct object ('his body') with the object marker را following the possessed noun; خویش is written as خوی + ش in the segmenting shown, so the ش is the possessive element 'his'. برکشید is simple past (3rd person singular) of برکشیدن, 'to lift/raise' — the verb comes at the end as in Persian, so the clause reads literally 'manfully his body he raised.' This is a concise, somewhat poetic past-tense construction.
segments.s438-g90: دلیر is a nominative/nominal subject or emphatic epithet 'the brave one'; it precedes the verb for emphasis. "از بن چاه" is a from‑phrase: از 'from', بن 'the bottom/base', چاه 'well' — together 'from the bottom of the well'. "بر سر کشید" combines the locative/goal phrase بر سر ('onto the top/rim') with کشید, past tense of کشیدن 'to pull/draw/haul' — so 'he pulled (himself) up onto the rim.' The verb is again past third‑person singular; the construction implies the subject of this clause (the brave man) pulled himself up from the well's bottom to its top.
segments.s438-g25: This is a conditional clause introduced by the archaic particle 'که‌گر' (if). The verbal phrase 'مست شد بنده' places the past-tense verb 'شد' (became) before the noun 'بنده' in a poetic inversion: 'the servant became intoxicated.' The prepositional phrase 'از بیهشی' (from/of stupor) gives the cause or state producing the intoxication.
segments.s438-g26: 'نمود' is a past-tense/past-stative verb meaning 'appeared/was shown.' The locative/adverbial 'اندران' ('therein'/'among them') locates the event, and 'بیهشی' names the state; together they modify where something appeared. 'سرکشی' here functions as a noun 'rebellion/waywardness' (the thing that showed itself).
segments.s438-g27: 'سزد‌گر' is an impersonal/conditional expression meaning 'if it is fitting' or 'should it be appropriate.' The verb 'ببخشی' is the classical present/subjunctive second-person form 'you forgive' (بـ- prefix + present stem), and 'گناه مرا' is the object: 'my sin' with the possessive clitic and the object marker split here into 'م' + 'را' (poetically separated).
segments.s438-g28: The verb 'کنی' is the subjunctive/present-second-person 'you make/do,' linking to the previous conditional ('if it befits ... you make'). 'تازه' modifies the coordinated noun phrase 'آیین و راه' ('custom/ritual and way/method'). The clitic cluster 'م ... را' again marks possession/object ('my ...'). Word order is verb then its object phrase in a classical poetic inversion.
segments.s438-g29: 'همی' is a frequentative/continuous marker ('kept on/was still'), followed by the past verb 'رفت' (went/advanced). 'پیشش' means 'before him/in his presence.' The phrase 'برهنه دو پای' is a poetic noun phrase 'with two bare feet' (adjective 'برهنه' precedes the noun group 'دو پای').
segments.s438-g30: This is a pair of parallel nominal phrases. 'سری پر ز کینه' = 'a head full of hatred' where 'پر' + 'ز' means 'full of.' The second phrase 'دلی پر ز رای' = 'a heart full of counsel/plan.' The nouns 'کینه' and 'رای' are the contents introduced by the poetic preposition 'ز' (from/of).
segments.s438-g31: 'ببخشید' is a past-tense verb 'forgave' (3rd singular) and, in this poetic word order, the subject 'رستم' follows the verb. 'گناه ورا' is the object phrase 'the sin for/to him' where 'ورا' functions as the dative/third-person clitic 'to him' (i.e., 'forgave him his sin' or 'forgave his sin').
segments.s438-g32: 'بیفزود' is a past-tense verb 'he added.' The demonstrative 'زان' ('from that'/'thereof') links this action to what preceded, and 'پایگاه ورا' is the thing given/added 'a base/place/status to him' with 'ورا' again marking the indirect object ('to him'). Word order places verb before its complements, as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s438-g33: بفرمود is a past, honorific third-person verb — 'he ordered/said'. It introduces a subordinate clause with تا plus the jussive/ subjunctive بپوشید (here used as a command/request: 'that he cover his head'). سر is the direct object 'head' and بپوشید is the jussive form of پوشیدن 'to cover/wear'. The coordinating و simply links the next noun phrase.
segments.s438-g34: به زین بر نشست is a locative/ directional phrase + verb: به (towards/onto) + زین (saddle) + بر (on/upon) with نشست (past 'sat'). The compound prepositional setup (به ... بر) yields 'sat upon the saddle.' و بیامد ز جای follows with بیامد (past 'came') used here with ز جای ('from his place') to mean 'rose/moved from his seat.'
segments.s438-g35: بر شهر کابل is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the city Kabul' placed before the verb for emphasis. یکی جای بود is an indefinite existential construction: یکی ('a certain') + جای ('place') + بود (past 'was') = 'there was a certain place.'
segments.s438-g36: ز سبزی زمینش is a descriptive prepositional phrase 'from/for the greenness of its land' modifying the place. دلارای is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'pleasing/charming,' and بود is the copular past 'was' linking the description: 'it was delightful because of the greenness of its land.'
segments.s438-g37: بدو is anaphoric dative 'to it' (referring back to the place). اندرون چشمه بود is 'inside, a spring was' — the usual classical word order places the locative phrase before the copula: 'within it there was a spring.' The following و درخت prepares the continuation 'and trees...'.
segments.s438-g38: به شادی نهادند is an active past plural verb phrase: به شادی ('for joy' / 'to celebrate') + نهادند (they placed/ set). هرجای تخت 'in every place of the throne' functions as the locative complement; overall: 'they planted/placed trees about every spot of the throne for festivity.' Note the verb carries an unspecified plural agent (people arranging the place).
segments.s438-g39: This clause uses fronting/inversion common in epic style: بسی خوردنیها (a great many foods) appears before the verb; بیاورد is the past narrative 'brought'; شاه following it is the subject 'the king.' The word بسی here emphasizes large quantity ('much/many').
segments.s438-g40: بیاراست is a past-tense verb 'he adorned/arranged.' Its object is the noun phrase خرم یکی جشنگاه: خرم (an adjective 'joyful' or 'pleasant') modifies یکی جشنگاه ('a certain place of celebration'). The adjective precedes the noun in this poetic word order.
segments.s438-g17: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. چشمش is a possessed noun 'his eye' (literal eye/look) and is the subject of رسید. به + روی is the prepositional phrase 'upon the face (of)'—به here marks direction/toward—and تهمتن is the name/epithet in the object position of that prepositional phrase. رسید is a past tense verb 'reached/fell upon' meaning the eye landed on that face.
segments.s438-g18: پیاده شد is a common Persian compound meaning 'he dismounted' (literally 'became on-foot'): پیاده is 'on foot' and شد is the past-tense auxiliary. از باره is a prepositional phrase 'from the baggage/pack/horse' (here: 'from his mount/saddle'); را is the object/topic marker attached to کو (here functioning interrogatively/indefinitely), and بدید is the past tense 'he saw.' The clause orders action after the temporal clause: he dismounted and then saw.
segments.s438-g19: ز here is the poetic form 'from/of'. سرشاره functions as an attributive compound meaning 'abundant/richly adorned (head-thing)' describing the head-covering; هندوی qualifies it as 'Indian' (i.e. of the Hindus/India). برگرفت is a past-tense verb 'took up/removed' — so the phrase means he removed the Indian head-covering.
segments.s438-g20: برهنه شد is 'became naked/unclothed' (برهنه = bare + شد auxiliary). و links the next action: دست بر سر گرفت is an idiomatic sequence 'took/laid his hands upon his head' (دست = hand, بر = on/upon, سر = head, گرفت = took/seized) signalling a gesture of grief.
segments.s438-g21: همان is the demonstrative 'that' (pointing to the slipper). موزه here is the regional/poetic word for a shoe/slipper; از پای بیرون کشید is a verb phrase 'pulled out from the foot' (از = from, پای = foot, بیرون = out, کشید = pulled/drew). The past verb governs the whole action: he removed the slipper.
segments.s438-g22: به زاری 'with lamentation/in grief' (به = with/to, زاری = lamentation). ز + مژگان = 'from the eyelashes' (z = from, مژگان = eyelashes). همی is the older continuous/iterative marker 'was/kept (doing)'. خون کشید here means 'blood was drawn/flowed' — خون = blood; کشید is the past of کشیدن ('to draw/pull' or 'to flow' in idiomatic use), so 'he shed/drew blood from his eyelashes' (poetic way to say tears of blood). The specific verb sense 'to pull/draw' is not among the provided sense options, so use -1 for کشید.
segments.s438-g23: دو رخ را is the direct object 'both faces/cheeks' (دو = two, رخ = face; را marks the definite object). به خاک سیه بر نهاد is a classical phrasing 'placed upon the black earth' (به = onto/toward, خاک سیه = black earth/dust, بر نهاد = put/laid upon — نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place'). The whole clause describes prostration of the face to the ground.
segments.s438-g24: همی کرد is a continuous-past construction 'he kept doing / he did' (همی marking ongoing aspect, کرد = did/made). پوزش is 'an apology/excuse' and ز کار شغاد is a prepositional phrase 'for/from the deed of Shaghad' (ز = because/of/from, کار = deed/action). The final word شغاد is a proper name here (Shaghad) and is not matched by the provided lexical senses, so its sense is marked -1.
segments.s438-g57: This is a pair of prepositional phrases naming animals in their domains. The preposition به introduces the location/domain (‘in/on’ or ‘to’ the sea/plain) followed by the noun that names that domain and then the animal that belongs there. The two halves are joined by و (‘and’) and the word order is domain → animal (a poetic, terser listing).
segments.s438-g58: همان here is a demonstrative linking this item to the previous list (‘likewise’/‘that same’). شیر is the head noun followed by two postpositive epithets جنگاور and تیزچنگ (noun + adjectives follow in Persian poetry). There is no copula; the adjectives immediately qualify the lion.
segments.s438-g59: ابا begins a correlative concessive pair meaning roughly ‘be it…’ or ‘whether…’; it introduces the contrast between a tiny creature and a giant one. پشه and پیل are the contrasted nouns, و joins them, and در چنگ مرگ is a fixed prepositional phrase ‘in the claw/grip of Death’ (در = in, چنگ = claw/grip, مرگ = Death).
segments.s438-g60: یکی باشد functions idiomatically as ‘(they are) one/indistinguishable’ (one and the same). ایدر = ‘here/in this place,’ followed by بدن (‘body’) as topic and نیست (‘is not/there is no’) negating برگ (‘leaf’). The clause is terse and metaphoric: no ‘leaf’ remains for the body (a poetic way to say no remnant/survival).
segments.s438-g61: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past ‘he commanded/said’. تا introduces a purpose or subordinate clause (‘that/so that’) and is followed by the subjunctive plural کنند (‘they do/make’) — a standard classical construction: ‘he ordered that they saddle his steed’ (رخش را زین = object marked by را: ‘saddle his steed’).
segments.s438-g62: همه here quantifies the result: ‘all/the whole (plain)’. دشت is the place ‘plain’ and پر + (باز و شاهین) is a verbless descriptive phrase meaning ‘full of hawks and falcons’ — پر is used as ‘full (of)’ and is followed by the nouns باز and شاهین. The verb کنند at the end links this result back to the ordering verb: ‘they made the plain full of hawks and falcons.’
segments.s438-g63: کمان کیانی is a noun plus an epithet/adjective (Kayanian = royal). به زه بر نهاد is a causative/placement phrase with نهاد (past of نهادن = ‘placed/put’) as the main verb: literally ‘placed the bow onto the string’ i.e. ‘he strung the royal bow’ (به + زه indicate the thing placed with/on the string).
segments.s438-g64: همی راند is narrative past (‘he rode/drove’ — همی gives an ongoing/narrative coloring). بر دشت = ‘across/on the plain.’ او refers back to the horse (رخش) and is the object being ridden. با شغاد (‘with darts/arrows’) specifies the means/arms used while riding. Word order: verb (راند) followed by locative and object phrases; ‘با شغاد’ is an instrumental phrase.
segments.s438-g41: می‌آورد is the imperfect/present-stem narrative ('he would bring' / 'he brought') with the habitual/past-narrative flavor common in epic Persian. و simply links two actions ('and'). رامشگران را uses را as the definite-object/emphasis marker; رامشگران here means the performers/entertainers called to amuse the guests. بخواند is the third-person past/subjunctive-form used here to mean 'called/summoned' (literally 'read/call'), so the clause means 'he brought (them) and called in the entertainers.'
segments.s438-g42: مهان را is 'the guests' with را marking them as the definite direct object. به + تخت مهی is the prepositional phrase 'to/on a moon-like / resplendent throne' (مهی is a poetic descriptor). بر نشاند is a compound verbal idea: بر (on/upon) + نشاند (archaic past of 'to seat/put'); together they mean 'set/seat (someone) upon.'
segments.s438-g43: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon'; به رستم marks the indirect object 'to Rostam' with the preposition به; چنین گفت شاه literally orders the word order unusually (object/phrase before verb) and means 'thus the king spoke' or 'the king said thus.' Note the verb گفت is the simple past 'said' used in narrative.
segments.s438-g44: که introduces reported speech ('that'). چون here functions temporally as 'when' (classical/poetic usage). رایت آید is 'the banner/standard comes' (آید is the present/nominalized narrative form used for a future/impending event). به نخچیرگاه = 'to/at the hunting-ground' (نخچیرگاه = place for hunting).
segments.s438-g45: یکی جای دارم = 'I have a certain/a particular place' (یکی used as 'a certain'). برین دشت و کوه is a poetic locative phrase 'in/among these plains and mountains' — برین is a contracted/poetic form combining بر (on/in) + این (this); word order is emphatic (place specified after 'have').
segments.s438-g46: به هر جای = 'to every place'; نخچیر گشته گروه places the perfect-participial phrase 'نخچیر گشته' ('where the quarry/game has gone' or 'turned into a hunting-place') before the subject/group marker گروه at the end — a common Persian word order (circumstance + verb/participle + subject). Together: 'a band/group has gone to every hunting-place.'
segments.s438-g47: همه دشت غرمست is an emphatic topical clause: 'the entire plain is غرمست' (a poetic adjective meaning exuberant/proudly intoxicated — here 'in high spirits' or 'ringing with activity'). و آهو و گور lists the quarry animals (آهو deer/gazelle, گور the onager/wild ass), linked to the state of the plain.
segments.s438-g48: کسی را که باشد تگاور ستور is an idiomatic-style line whose literal pieces are: کسی را (as for any person), که باشد (who is/should be — باشد is the classical/polite present/subjunctive of 'to be'), تگاور ستور = 'a rider (تگاور) of a steed (ستور)'. The intended meaning in context is existential/generalizing: 'there is no one (or no one present) who is not a mounted rider,' i.e. everyone there is a horseman. Grammatically, را on کسی gives topical/emphatic force, and the clause uses classical subjunctive/present form باشد to state a general condition.
segments.s438-g1: بداختر is an epithet literally 'ill‑starred' used as a noun/adjective; چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. از شهر کابل is a standard prepositional phrase 'from the city of Kabul'. برفت is a past‑tense third‑person singular verb 'went away/left' (an older/poetic form with the prefix بر‑). The clause is a temporal subordinate 'When X left...' that links to the result in the next segment.
segments.s438-g2: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + آن' meaning 'in/at that' (locative pointer). دشت نخچیرگاه is a noun phrase 'the plain, the hunting‑ground' (دشت modified by نخچیرگاه). شد is the past intransitive 'became' (here marking a change of state). شاه appears after the verb for emphasis and is the subject 'the king'; تفت is a terse/poetic element whose exact sense is unclear in isolation (likely part of an epithet or a name) and therefore remains ambiguous here. Overall: 'In that plain the king became (game/prey...).'
segments.s438-g3: ببرد is a past form of بردن here meaning 'he took/carried off'. از میان is the fixed phrase 'from among'. لشکری is an adjectival/attributive form 'of the soldiery / among the troops'. چاه‑کن is the direct object 'a well‑digger' (an agent noun). Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and object, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s438-g4: کجا is the interrogative 'where' (here rhetorical). نام بردند is the compound action 'نام بردن' = 'to name/mention', with بردند as past‑tense 'they mentioned/named'. زان is 'از + آن' = 'from that / of that' pointing back to the preceding matter. انجمن is 'assembly / gathering'. The clause asks rhetorically where that assembly was not spoken of (i.e. it was named everywhere).
segments.s438-g5: سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout' or 'all over'. همه reinforces 'all / every'. دشت is 'plain' and نخچیرگاه is the compound noun 'hunting‑ground' functioning as the head noun. The phrase means 'throughout the whole hunting plain.'
segments.s438-g6: همه again 'all/everyone' is the subject. چاه here is 'pit / trap' (not just a water‑well) and بد is the adjective 'bad / evil' qualifying the pits. کنده is the perfect/past participle form of کندن 'dug' ('had dug'). در زیر راه is a locative phrase 'under the road / beneath the way'. The whole unit says that everyone had dug wicked pits under the path.
segments.s438-g7: زده is the past participle/verb 'struck / set / stuck' (here 'set into'). حربه‑ها is the plural 'weapons' and را is the direct‑object marker emphasizing them. بن اندر زمین is a poetic locative: بن (base/bottom) + اندر زمین 'in the earth' = 'into the ground / in the earth'. Together: 'they had stuck the weapons into the ground.'
segments.s438-g8: همان نیز is 'the same / those likewise' (anaphoric 'also'). ژوپین is a loan/technical term for a padded coat or gambeson (armour). و is 'and'. شمشیر is 'sword' and کین is a post‑nominal modifier 'of vengeance / hateful', giving 'swords of vengeance' or 'vengeful swords'. This short clause lists additional martial items found there.
segments.s438-g65: زوراه (Zawareh) here names the destination/place and appears without a preposition as a locative noun; همی + رفت is the epic past/imperfective verbal phrase meaning “went” or “kept going” (همی marks ongoing/past habitual aspect). با introduces the companion (with), and پیلتن is treated as a place-name or proper noun accompanying the verb.
segments.s438-g66: تنی چند is an idiom meaning ‘a few (persons)’ (literally ‘some bodies’); ازان (= از + آن) links those few to something mentioned earlier (‘of that’); نامدار انجمن is a noun phrase ‘renowned assembly/company’ describing whom they are. Word order places the small-number phrase first, then the genitive link, then the describing noun phrase.
segments.s438-g67: به نخچیر is a prepositional/phrasal expression of purpose or motive — ‘for the spoil/booty’; لشکر is the subject ‘the army/host’; پراگنده شد is the past tense idiom ‘became scattered’ (شد as intransitive auxiliary meaning ‘became’). The clause therefore states the army broke up to seize plunder.
segments.s438-g68: اگر here functions not strictly as a modern conditional but as a connective introducing a simultaneous/secondary action (often glossed in poetry as ‘and/when’); کنده‌گر is an agent noun ‘the digger/wood‑cutter’ (a person). سوی + آگنده expresses a direction/tendency (‘toward the آگنده’), and شد is the past auxiliary ‘became/turned’. In context this reads as ‘and the digger turned/moved toward the blazing/filled place’ (آگنده used poetically).
segments.s438-g69: زواره names the place (to/at Zawareh); تهمتن is the proper name ‘Tahmätan’ and is the grammatical subject; بران is a locative/adverbial particle ‘on/thereon’ (pointing back to that road), and راه بود is the copular phrase ‘was on the road’ (بود = past ‘was’). The clause therefore states that Tahmätan was on that route to Zawareh.
segments.s438-g70: ز بهر زمان is a temporal/teleological phrase ‘for a while/for the sake of the time’ (ز بهر = ‘for’/‘because of’ + زمان ‘time’). کاندران modifies چاه and is a less transparent word here (poetically indicating a particular kind of pit or thing in the pit); بود is the past copula ‘there was’. Together: ‘for a while there was a کاندران pit there’ — the exact sense of کاندران is obscure in the diction and likely poetic/technical.
segments.s438-g71: همی + می‌یافت is the habitual/continuous past (‘kept finding’ / ‘kept catching’); رخش is the steed/warhorse as subject; زان خاک is an ablative/adverbial phrase ‘from that dust/earth’ (زان = from that); بوی is the direct object ‘scent’. Word order places the object noun after the verb for emphasis but the verb still governs the object.
segments.s438-g72: تن خویش را is a direct object phrase ‘his own body’ (تن = body; خوی + ش = ‘one’s own’ with ش the possessive suffix; را marks it as definite object). کرد is the past verb ‘made/did’ (here ‘made/turned [his body]’). چون + گردگوی is a simile ‘like a globe/round ball’. Altogether: ‘he made/rolled his body like a sphere,’ describing a sudden compacting/rolling movement.
segments.s438-g73: همی is the epic/habitual-progressive marker meaning 'kept/was continually'. جست is the past of جستن 'to leap'. و simply links actions 'and'. ترسان شد is an adjectival + شد construction: ترسان 'frightened/afraid' + شد 'became' = 'became frightened'. از here is causal 'because of/at'. بوی خاک is a genitival phrase 'the smell of the earth' (بوی = ‘smell’ + خاک = ‘earth’). The whole unit reads 'he kept leaping and became frightened by the smell of the earth.'
segments.s438-g74: زمین را is the definite object (را marks زمین as the affected thing). به + نعلش functions instrumentally here: 'with his horseshoe' (نعل + possessive -ش). همی کرد is the habitual/past compound 'kept making/was making' (همی progressive + کرد 'made/did'). چاک is the resultant noun 'a gash/split'—'to make a gash' is expressed as کرد چاک ('made a split').
segments.s438-g75: بزد is literally 'struck' but with گام ('step') forms the idiom 'set/took a step' — i.e. he moved forward. گام is 'step'. رخش تگاور reads as 'the rider of Rakhsh' (رخش = the steed, تگاور = the rider/warrior). به راه 'on/into the road' (به + راه giving direction). The verb-subject is omitted (typical epic terseness); the rider is the understood agent.
segments.s438-g76: چنین 'thus/so' links the manner to what follows. تا here is temporal 'until' meaning 'up to the point that'. بیامد is past 'came'. میان چاه 'between two pits/wells' (میان = between, چاه = pit/well). Together: 'thus, until he came between two pits.'
segments.s438-g77: دل رستم is a possessive NP 'Rostam's heart'. از in this clause expresses cause/target of emotion 'because of/at' (از رخش = 'at/with respect to his steed'). شد is 'became'. پر ز خشم is the phrase 'full of anger' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic 'from/of', خشم = 'anger').
segments.s438-g78: زمانش is 'time' with the possessive suffix -ش ('his/its/time'); here it works as the subject 'time' or 'the moment'. خرد را 'wisdom' is the definite object (را marks it). بپوشید is the simple past of پوشیدن with the prefix ب- (here 'covered/concealed'). چشم is 'eye' and is the thing being covered by time: 'time covered the eye of wisdom' (i.e. wisdom was obscured).
segments.s438-g79: یکی here is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one'. تازیانه is 'a whip'. برآورد in context is the verbal phrase 'lifted/raised' (classical/poetic use of برآوردن = to lift/raise up); this specific sense is not among the provided sense choices, so it is marked -1. نرم is an adverb/adjective 'gently' modifying the action of raising.
segments.s438-g80: بزد is past 'he struck' (the act of striking follows the raised whip). نیک‌دل here qualifies رخش: 'the kind‑hearted Rakhsh' (an epithet). رخش را: رخش is the direct object (را marks it). کرد گرم is a causative/result phrase 'made (him) warm/ardent' — idiomatically 'roused him, set him going' (کرد = 'made', گرم = 'fervent/energized').
segments.s438-g9: به marks the instrumental/material relation here ('with' or 'by'), خاشاک is the material 'chaff', and کرده is the past participle/past form of کردن meaning 'made/placed'. سر چاه کور is the noun phrase 'the head/top (mouth) of the blind well' with سر = 'top' modifying چاه = 'well' and کور = 'blind' qualifying the well. The whole phrase reads literally 'with chaff made the top of the blind well.'
segments.s438-g10: که introduces the following result/relative clause ('so that/which'); مردم ندیدی functions as a negative past clause 'people did not see' (classical/epic negative past form), and نه ... نه ... coordination is implied by the negation: نه چشم ستور = 'nor the eyes of beasts.' چشم is the organ 'eye' and ستور here means 'beasts/cattle (i.e. animals/steeds)'.
segments.s438-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the subject. دمان سر برفتن نهاد is an idiomatic verbal complex: دمان 'hem/skirt' + سر + the infinitive برفتن 'to depart' + نهاد (past of نهاد 'put/placed') — here نهاد + infinitive means 'made to / put (his) head to depart', i.e. 'set himself to leave' or 'prepared to depart.' Note the older verb برفتن used for 'to go/leave.'
segments.s438-g12: This clause has a poetic word order: object (سواری 'the rider') + verb (برافگند 'threw off' — past) + agent (پویان شغاد). برافگند is the past tense 'he/she/it flung off', and پویان شغاد functions as the agent phrase (here either an epithet + name or a compound epithet 'swift as a dart'). The literal reading is '(the) rider he flung off — Puyan Shaghad.'
segments.s438-g13: که introduces a reported clause linked to what precedes. آمد is the simple past 'came'. گو is the reportive particle 'they say/it is said' attached to the verb. پیلتن is a place name; بی سپاه means 'without an army' (بی = 'without', سپاه = 'army'). Altogether: 'which, it is said, came from Piltan without an army.'
segments.s438-g14: بیا is the imperative 'come!' and پیش here means 'before/in the presence (of someone)'. وزان ('and/from that/thereupon') links this command to the previous event; کرده is the perfective/past participle 'having made/after making' and introduces the following participial construction, while زنهار خواه is a collocation: زنهار 'warning/admonition' and خواه (from خواستن) 'ask/seek' used in an injunctional sense — 'warn and beg' or 'having warned, beg for mercy.' The line thus issues two imperatives: 'Come forward, and having warned, beg.'
segments.s438-g15: سپهدار کابل is a title + place genitive 'the commander of Kabul.' بیامد is the past 'came', and ز + شهر is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the city.' Word order is straightforward: 'The commander of Kabul came from the city.'
segments.s438-g16: This is a pair of descriptive noun phrases joined to describe the commander: زبان پرسخن is 'tongue full of speech' i.e. 'loquacious of tongue' (پرسخن is an adjectival/compound form meaning 'much-speaking' or 'talkative'). دل پر از + nouns uses پر 'full' + از 'of' to mean 'heart full of (کین و زهر)'. The line uses juxtaposed nominal phrases instead of finite verbs — a common poetic device for compact character description.

sentences.s438-s25: On the plain the onager and the gazelle fall into his claw; one cannot pass through that lush plain.
sentences.s438-s26: Because of his words Rostam became enraged; that plain was full of water and of grave-robbers.
sentences.s438-s27: When something befalls someone, time may confuse him, and his heart's perception becomes blind, one would suppose.
sentences.s438-s28: Such is the way of the world: the world will not reveal its hidden things to us.
sentences.s438-s41: When he became cramped between two pits, he kept seeking a way out of the clutch of Fortune.
sentences.s438-s42: Both his feet sank into a single pit; there was no place for hanging or for battle.
sentences.s438-s43: The bottom of the pit was full of weapons and sharp swords; there was no place for a man nor any way of escape.
sentences.s438-s44: They tore the flank of the mighty Rakhsh, and cut the arm and foot of that great champion.
sentences.s438-s45: Manfully he heaved up his body; the brave man hauled himself from the bottom of the well to its rim.
sentences.s438-s13: If the servant became drunk from stupor,
sentences.s438-s14: If it befits (you) to forgive my sin and renew my custom and way,
sentences.s438-s15: he kept going before him with both feet bare, a head full of hatred, a heart full of counsel,
sentences.s438-s16: Rostam forgave his sin and added to him that rank/place of standing.
sentences.s438-s17: He ordered that his head be covered, put his foot in the stirrup, sat upon the saddle, and rose from his place.
sentences.s438-s18: Above the city of Kabul there was a certain place; because of the greenness of its land it was delightful.
sentences.s438-s19: Within it was a spring, and trees were set for rejoicing in every place around the throne.
sentences.s438-s20: The king brought much food and adorned a joyful place of celebration.
sentences.s438-s9: When his eye fell on Tahmatan's face, he dismounted; from his mount he saw who it was.
sentences.s438-s10: He lifted off the Indian, richly decorated head-covering; he became naked and put his hands to his head.
sentences.s438-s11: He pulled that slipper off his foot; with lamentation, blood flowed from his eyelashes.
sentences.s438-s12: He laid his two cheeks upon the black earth; he made apology for Shaghad's deed.
sentences.s438-s29: In the sea a whale, and on the plain a leopard; likewise the lion, warlike and sharp‑clawed.
sentences.s438-s30: Be it gnat or elephant in Death’s claw, they are the same here — the body has no leaf.
sentences.s438-s31: He ordered that his steed be saddled; they filled the plain with hawks and falcons.
sentences.s438-s32: He strung the Kayanian bow; then he rode him across the plain with darts.
sentences.s438-s21: He brought them and summoned the entertainers; he set the guests upon a moon-like (splendid) throne.
sentences.s438-s22: After that the king said to Rostam thus: that when the banner comes to the hunting-ground...
sentences.s438-s23: I have a certain spot in these plains and hills; to every hunting-place a band has gone (i.e., parties have spread to every place of game).
sentences.s438-s24: The whole plain is elated, and the gazelles and onagers — there is not a person who is not a horseman with a steed.
sentences.s438-s1: When the ill‑starred one left the city of Kabul, in that plain the king became prey.
sentences.s438-s2: He carried off from among the troops a well‑digger; where did they not mention that assembly?
sentences.s438-s3: Throughout the entire hunting plain, everyone had dug wicked pits beneath the road.
sentences.s438-s4: They had thrust their weapons into the ground; likewise there were gambesons and swords of vengeance.
sentences.s438-s33: They went to Zawareh with Piltan, a few men of that famed company.
sentences.s438-s34: The host scattered for the spoil, and the digger turned toward the blazing (place).
sentences.s438-s35: Tahmätan was on that road to Zawareh; for a time there was an obscure (or peculiar) pit there.
sentences.s438-s36: His steed kept getting the scent of that dust; he turned his body like a round ball.
sentences.s438-s37: He kept leaping and, frightened by the smell of the earth, he kept tearing the ground with his horseshoe.
sentences.s438-s38: The rider of Rakhsh set a step upon the road, and thus came until he was between two pits.
sentences.s438-s39: Rostam's heart became full of anger at Rakhsh; time covered the eye of wisdom.
sentences.s438-s40: Someone gently lifted a whip and struck; he hit the kind‑hearted Rakhsh and roused him.
sentences.s438-s5: He had the mouth of the blind well filled with chaff, so that neither people nor the eyes of beasts could see it.
sentences.s438-s6: When Rostam prepared to set out, the (swift) Shaghad flung off the rider.
sentences.s438-s7: Which, it is said, came from Piltan without an army; come forward, and having so warned, beg (for mercy).
sentences.s438-s8: The commander of Kabul came from the city — loquacious of tongue, his heart full of hatred and venom.
